headlines.title: Fort Worth Man Addresses Toronto Ad Men's Convention
headlines.subtitle: A. L. Shuman Talks on "Creating Interest in Advertising Through the News Columns"- Notes of Fort Worth Party.
headlines.fullText:
BY JAMES R. RECORD, Staff Correspondent With Ad Men. Special to The Star.Teleorus TORONTO, June 24 .- A. L. Shuman advertising manager of The tsar-Tele- gram addressed today's convention of the Associated Advertising Clubs of Ing Interest In Advertising Through the News Columns." Shuman said: On the title page of a little periodica! that comes to my desk each month 1 recently noticed this injunction: "It Is not necessary to teach men-it is nee- essary to reach men." This expression struck me as being singularly apropos of the idea of developing interest In ad- vertising through the news columns of a daily paper, as this feature appealed to me. I have always thought that per- haps a relatively small percentage of readers are advertisers or are inter- eated in advertising and carefully read advertising matter. But how are we to reach those who belong to netther class mentioned, those who are, to say the least Indifferent to the printed word as expressed in the advertising of the man who has a commodity to sell.
True, we have had sundry kinds of advertising talks in hundreds of daily newspapers throughout the land, but these are usually syndicated and set In a display boken some corner, or the for tall tors, and writers of these talks, and educators, they do motoros ah the full purpose for which they are writ- ten. They do not reach the men and women who are indifferent.
Every newspaper has an editorial page, within the limitations of which it is the mission of the newspaper to en- deavor to teach, instruct and mold the opinions of its readers with reference to the current topics of the day. That these editorial expressions of the pub- lishers accomplish this end cannot be calnaald-more battles have been wod and lost as a result of forceful edi- Horas than in any other way. Po- made-controversies ended-great have been begun and ended sret movements have been cambletten by the powerful influence of well written editorials, upon the minds editorial usewapaper. The much heralded "power of the press" is contained within the bounds of the editorial pages.
Granting that these facts are true, and I hardly think anyone will dispute the statements made herein, why should not every newspaper have editorials of instruction on business and advertis- ing topics, not syndicated and boxed as display matter, but given the same editorial pages that is now accorded expressions of the publisher on other subjects?
This manner of appeal will direct It- se foreldrene han meses nad vertising and it will especially reach the male member of the family, who is naturally less thrifty than the wom- an, and is consequently an indifferent reader of advertising and comparative- ly a poor shopper. Convert the man through the advertising editorial: he has been converted to many policies and many habits through the other edi- torial pages. Educate him to the level where he will not buy the first brand of shirt that is thrust under his nose, whether it be an advertised brand er not: tutcate motownone tain better values for his money by confining his purchases to advertised commodities Every man here knows that one great purpose of advertising is to decrease fully small percentage of the buying publle is aware of or realize this im- portant fact.
If the man and his wife have thrust before them each day, or each week, short, newsy, human interest editorials of how other familles are decreasing the cost of living by watching for sales of needed merchandise, would they not be Impressed? Testimonials for the ad- vertising cultural bemun emd thinke cured and the pubne putem and then read the advan e more in avere month to ten ite supremacy in aver- units reichtso far as it goes, if Watch is au remove dorde en ampen of space to advertising stories and editoriais in newsy, readable form. the cuma cause of advertising in ita entirety would be benefited to an im- measurably great extent and more ad- metsing would accrue to every legiti- mate medium as a result of such an educational campaign.
With this purpose in mind, the "Ad- vertisin" Spizzerinktum" feature of the Sunday Issue of the Fort Worth Star- Telegram had its Inception, with your speaker as the editor. A word in ex- planation of the bomenentre feature will not be amiss at this time, particularly with reference to the word spizzerianam ot banens tarde Bu Webster. book compiled by the late Mr. Webster, I noted tabacummarcomed Cumaity led to a second glance, and budemevery that the definition was or the overpowering will "vim, energy In other words, what is commonly termed in the baseball comm =>- This definition was 80 strongly expressive of the spirit that prevailed throughout the entire Star- Telegram organization, that the word was adopted as a slogan, and in our city and state has since become synonomous with the activities of the Star-Tele- gram. Hence the use of the word in connection with the advertising edi- toplat feature of the paper. Since Its Inception the Advertising Spizzerinktum column of the Star. Telegram has developed a wonderful amount of added Interest In advertis- Inã Ger como indifferent men arvertising has been doubled, a thirty-horse power motor has been made into one of sixty-horse Some way other for development purposes and for education as to ad- Dufrene values.
About two columns are devoted to be feature in each Sunday's issue. Ex- this featurewe pressofelted from the wading advertising men of the coun- try, to which is added short, crisp. hu- man Interest stories, with local flavor. thus making expressions appeal to the thrift of the consumer, as well as to tend to educate the layman to the windom of judicious advertising of his waren, To my mind, every newspaper in the land should intensity interest in ad- ersins .... developing That. Interest all classes can be reached in this way -many of whom will not give atten- tion to any other kind of appeal. It has been my experience in Fort Worth, since this feature has been used in the Star-Telegram to note increasing in- terest manifested by those who have, heretofore been impervious to the ap- peais of the molleitor. The advertising editorial paves the way for the space meller to drive home the clinching ar- gument, which transforms a ponad- vertiser into dott are naturally skeptical and these doubt- etal aseredited benennen vertising, Lack of confidence in all publicity has been the cry of auch mnen. As a consequence they read no adver. tising whatever. But piace advertising news before this class and interest in nearly always aroused, perhaps not im- mediately, but invariably they will find something in such news to catch the attention. After the entering wedge it is but a matter of time until all bar- riers are broken. With proper follow- up methods, your prospect can soon be made to fuite the great possibility in advertising as a business builder. Incorporated within these advertising editorial campaigns should be well laid plans for the development of various classes of advertising in which little or no interest 's manttested at this time.
Personally. I Have been on some ppent using considerable space in an appeal has pot cal conditions, this has, so far, not re- ever, the lee has been broken and the parution have appointed committees to confer with me and ob- tain details as to the cost of the plan. Unfortunately, our pastors' associations are denominational and each maintains Its separate body. If we had one cen- tralized organisation, composed of rep- resentatives of all denominations, there can be no doubt but that the plan would have gone through long ago, but having to deal with so many different bodies has delayed the mariate terne short confident, however, that within a sport Worth will be using a page each week as the result of . achten tion of the leaders in the various churches through advertising editorials in the news columns.
There is really no limitation to the mens method of appear not permit going into further detail. It is a feature which I firmly believe can be made to do more for the de- velopment of advertising than any other manner in which the reader can be renched. I have tried to sow the need at this meeting today. Some of you may be already using a similar plan in your own publications. If there are any here who have not adopted this paper: 1 May ta hine et paper. I say to him, let that be your first move When you reben tart ness of your advertising editorial er Bewa columns by permitting them to become permet Hbc the personal, human Interest annesi that gets close to the consumer and causes a carping eritie to become a candid convert.

headlines.title: ROTARIANS REPORT FT. WORTH'S SUCCESS
headlines.subtitle: City Had More Delegates in Hall Monday Than Did Houston Itself.
headlines.fullText:
Fort Worth Rotarians who returned home Tuesday on their special train from the international convention of Rotary Clubs at Houston told Wednes- day how the convention made a com- plate surrender to the Panther City dalegation.
"Some of the Rotarians from far Northern and Eastern cities," Marion Bansom said, "could not get it out of their heads that Fort Worth was for after the next convention nor after anything but the good will of the vist- tors from other states
Fort Worth's delegation of more than Blo was by far the biggest at the con- be more Rotarians in the convention Sin Monday then Houston Itself.
Marthe Fort Worth Rotarians re- hedertherepedal, but a few re- mained in Houston. They will accom- pany the Rotarians of other cities on the circle tour of the state, spending Best Monday in Fort Worth. According to those who returned on the special train, the speech of W. B. Paddock of Fort Worth welcoming Ine visitors to Houston on behalf of the Rotary Clubs of Texas was the hit of the opening session of the convention. Several of the Fort Worth Rotary party, Including the wives delsomthe the members, came home bremen result of eating too much crab sum while they were near the sea. Still more of the Fort worth Itorarne were reported sick in Houston - feature "at supper given Syl: Monday was the upper "men at ayle van Beach by Houston Rotary Club
The full list of the Fort Worth par- ty follows: Hugh Jamieson, L. A. Rohrer, Mrs. A. L. Shuman, Mrs. M. E. Tadlock, Miss Minnie Williams, Mrs. W. T. Burch, Mrs. Milton H. Beck- er. C. H. Walton, Miss Elisabeth McDonald, Mrs. C. L Prouty, Mrs. R. O. MeCormack, Mrs. optis Barney Burns, H. R. Elbert, Mrs. Har- ney Buffs. R. O. MccormackTuny Bostick, Parke me E. T. Dimmick, Milton B. Becker, Mrs. Alice Mipe, Margaret Conner, G. W. are KathMiss Camilla Rea, Mrs. J. W. Buum w. W. Snider. and son, Mrs. Elmer Mitchell, Jack Toy. C. A. Reyer. Frederick Houle, Joe'A. Vera, Mrs. Amon G. Carter, Miss Bertice Carter, A. G. Carter, Tom Tay- Bor. I. N. Hopkins and wife, T. C. Gray. K. V. Stewart, Charles D. Coffey. Clinton Isnae Jr., H. C. Burke Jr. A Oppenheimer. W. B. Wilmans. C Walton, Miss Grace Franc Ferris, A. L. Shuman, George W. Bryant. E. R. Henry. L. E Brown, Harry Robinson, Chester Toy. StanleyMe- Gauntt, Hugh Jamieson, Rob Roy Me- Donald, Barney Burns, J. W. Exum. E. T. Dimmick.
Notes on the Trip. Detroit had lots of fun out of the "bulls" aboard the Fort Worth train. The afternoon papers carried special stories on the two "blooded" animals, their negro keeper, "Flip" Diggs, and their special car. These bulls were carried, the stories said, to demonstrate the transition of the Lone Star state from the long horn stage to The regis tered, blooded stage in stock raising. On Sunday morning, however, the dailles followed with the story that the bulls had been kidnapped along with their African keeper by the Waco Ad Club and taken captives to Toronto.
Of course, the bulls were not cap- tured after all-they reposed quietly in their wood erates all the time the Ad Clubbers were in Detroit. These "bulls" were donated to the tourists by Milt Eppstein and after their Canadian four- ney will be returned to the park of the Eppstein building on Throckmorton street.
Dennis also will be returned-to Jamieson & Diggs.
Rabbi George Fox met the tourists In Chicago and helped entertain them for several hours. The pastor of Tem- ple Beth-El is taking a summer course at Chicago University. Other former Fort Worth men who welcomed the Texans In Chicago were Paul Palmer. at one time assistant secretary of the how office manager for a Chicago Both DE Maneraelsenebenan Ba ert Wiley, ex-member of The'Star- casa now of the Chicago Journal, and Perley Boone once city editor of the Record and now assistant city editor of the Chi- cago Tribune.
H. C. Carson, Southwestern passen- ger agent of the New York Central lines, is looking after the return rail- road accommodations of the Fort marta Daten was from Fort Werte why assets codefest Worth and was especially solicitous in Detroit, where the train was parked all night.
Paul Smith of the Lozier and Presi- dent Briggs of the Briggs-Detroiter uation at their luncheon in honor of the Fort Worth party in Detroit. The hosts had understood their guests would be exclusively men, had prepared menu, speeches, etc., accordingly, but the embarrassment was turned into a good Joke, it was explained, perhaps Jokes were slightly amended and the affair was one of the most success- ful of the trip.
Tom Taylor "conducted" the special over part of the trip. Now Tom is a over store in Fort Worth-but it he ever wants to take up railroading he'll only have to don a cap. He's the best little "conductor" in the land.

headlines.title: 144 HAVE APPLIED FOR NEW LIQUOR LICENSES
headlines.subtitle: Majority of Permits Must Be Renewed Here Before July 10.
headlines.fullText: Applications for retali liquor deal- detall e. ibls part of the time of the clerk county court. A large number of the local licenses expire July 10 and applications have to be in ten days before the date of expiration. There are now 144 applications pend- ne. my presenting practically all of the llenses which expire at this time. Al- together there are 183 licenses held in the county.

headlines.title: NEGRO DIES IN FIELD; WOMAN IS FOUND DEAD
headlines.subtitle: Inquests in Both Cases Show Deaths Resulted From Natural Results.
headlines.fullText: Inquesta over the bodies of two ne- groes who died suddenl, were held on Wednesday by Justice Maben. R. Tay- lor, who was working on the John war- gess pince near Haslet, dropped dead There Tuesday afternoon and Anna home of L. A. Holmes, In Arlington founa asta , Anton Heights, was found dead In her room there Wednesday morning. It was decided death was due to mat- ural causes in both cases.

headlines.title: FLOODS MAY CAUSE RESERVOIR DISPUTE
headlines.subtitle: Floating of Stumps on to Cleared Ground Expected to Bring Further Trouble.
headlines.fullText:
Recent floods threaten to bring an- other difficulty into the dispute be- tween C. T. Hodge and the reservoir committee as to the amount of clear- Ing done under his contract for re- moving timber and brush from the die of Lake Worth.
The recent floods have brought much cut timber down the river and deposit- ec it along the banks. Some is said to have been deposited by the flood on cruund already prepared by Hodge fo Inspection by the city preparatory to fina action upon his claim. An estimate on the clearing by Meigs & Meigs has been requested in order to make a settlement on it.

He insists that the audit should be made to extend to all de- partments and not to the waterworks alone.

headlines.fullText:

headlines.title: Interest in Revival.
headlines.fullText: Much Interest Is being manifested in Badet Church. Ten Berry street, which are being conduct. Nutt of Gilmer. There have been twenty-two Tto the church so far. The revival will continue for several days longer, with meetings at 10 a. m. and 8 p. m.

headlines.title: BELIEVES WRONG MAN ARRESTED FOR THEFT
headlines.subtitle: Justice Releases Prisoner When Tie- groes Testify He Was Not One ef Robbers.
headlines.fullText: Convinced the wrong man had been me puedas after noon released Fred Gilbert, charged with highway robbery, on his own re- cognizance. A number of negroes tes- tifled positively that Gilbert was not cne of the robbers. According to their testimony the actual robbery was committed by a Mexican, assisted by two negroes, whose names they gave. "The negro who was robbed falled to appear for the hearing. One of the negro wemen who testified Tuesday said h .. told her he was going to leave. as he had been shooting "craps" with the men who robbed him,
